perturbation.original_text: 1.1 “Confidential Information” means; all technical, commercial, procurement requirements, purchasing, manufacturing, customer lists, investors, employees, business and contractual relationships, business forecasts, sales and merchandising, and marketing plans business or personnel information disclosed or otherwise made available in any format and/or physical manner by Grindrod SA or becoming available, before, during and/or after the execution of an interaction, duty or obligation including all information that makes itself known to the Vendor or comes into being as a result of the rendering, production and/or delivery of an agreement/understanding/request for quotation/contract or Purchase Order, or any other interaction. Confidential Information shall also include any other information that is marked as "Confidential" or should reasonably be considered confidential Confidential Information excludes information which is already in the possession, or under the control of the Vendor otherwise than as a result of having been disclosed by Grindrod SA to the Vendor or as a result of the preparation and execution of a proposal or any other interaction with Grindrod SA. Confidential Information also excludes information in the public domain for a reason, other than a breach of this Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Undertaking, with any party, or independently developed by the Vendor without reference to information provided by, Grindrod SA;
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 “Confidential Information” means only information explicitly marked as "Confidential" in writing by Grindrod SA. No other information, regardless of its nature or how it was obtained, shall be considered Confidential Information under this undertaking.
perturbation.explanation: This change narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information'. Originally, it encompassed a broad range of information, including that which should 'reasonably be considered confidential.' Now, it only includes information explicitly marked as such. This creates an in-text contradiction with Section 3.5 which refers to 'any abuse or unauthorized disclosure of such Confidential Information of which the Vendor becomes aware,' creating uncertainty about what constitutes an 'abuse or unauthorized disclosure' if the information isn't explicitly marked confidential, hence contradicting the use of 'such Confidential Information'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.5. to bring to the attention of Grindrod SA, in writing, any abuse or unauthorized disclosure of such Confidential Information of which the Vendor becomes aware,
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' to include only information explicitly marked as such, whereas the original definition was much broader and included information that should 'reasonably be considered confidential'. This creates a direct contradiction with Section 3.5, which refers to 'any abuse or unauthorized disclosure of such Confidential Information of which the Vendor becomes aware'. If only explicitly marked information is considered confidential, it becomes unclear what constitutes an 'abuse or unauthorized disclosure' of information that isn't explicitly marked, creating a strong in-text contradiction. The altered definition is less encompassing and creates confusion around the obligations stated in Section 3.5. The change breaks compatibility with the contradicted section.

perturbation.original_text: 3.3. not to disclose such Confidential Information to any person without the prior written authorization from Grindrod SA,
perturbation.changed_text: 3.3. not to disclose such Confidential Information to any person, except to its affiliates and subcontractors, without the prior written authorization from Grindrod SA,
perturbation.explanation: This change adds an exception to the non-disclosure obligation, allowing disclosure to affiliates and subcontractors without prior written authorization. This creates an in-text contradiction because previously any disclosure required authorization, but now certain entities are exempt. This change creates ambiguity and an in-text contradiction regarding the extent of the confidentiality obligations. Furthermore, the Vendor undertakes to use such Confidential Information only for purposes of performing as required in terms of its obligations / promises or duties arising out of the Agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1. to use such Confidential Information only for purposes of performing as required in terms of its obligations / promises or duties arising out of the Agreement / understanding /Request for Quotation / Contract or Purchase Order and for no other purpose whatsoever,
perturbation.justification: The original text required prior written authorization from Grindrod SA for *any* disclosure of confidential information. The changed text introduces an exception, allowing disclosure to affiliates and subcontractors without this prior authorization. Section 3.1 states that Confidential Information is only to be used for purposes of performing the Vendor's duties. The change creates a direct contradiction because now there is an exception to the initial obligation not to disclose the information to any person without authorization, and opens up the possibility of the information being disclosed to affiliates and subcontractors even if it's not related to performing the Vendor's duties, or even for purposes other than those related to the agreement.

perturbation.original_text: 1.6 “Vendor” means [Insert the full particulars of the Vendor, as per clause 1.2 above].
perturbation.changed_text: 1.6 “Vendor” means any individual or entity that provides goods or services to Grindrod SA, irrespective of whether a formal agreement is in place.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Vendor' refers to inserting the full particulars as per clause 1.2. The altered definition broadens it to any provider of goods or services, even without a formal agreement. This introduces an in-text contradiction because earlier clauses assume a formal agreement (e.g., 'Agreement / understanding / request for quotation / contract / order'). Now, someone providing a one-off service could be considered a 'Vendor' under this definition, yet the agreement clauses may not apply to them, causing ambiguity about who is bound by the NDA. Vendor may be required to quote on and / or render Deliverables to Grindrod SA.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Vendor and Grindrod SA agree that; 1. The Vendor and Grindrod SA have entered into an agreement / understanding / request for quotation / contract / order or any interaction of whatsoever nature, under which the Vendor may be required to quote on and / or render Deliverables to Grindrod SA and/or Grindrod SA shall be obliged to disclose or otherwise make available to the Vendor, Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the definition of 'Vendor' to include any provider of goods or services, irrespective of a formal agreement. The contradicted text explicitly states that 'The Vendor and Grindrod SA have entered into an agreement'. This creates a direct conflict, as the contradicted text assumes a formal agreement is in place, whereas the changed definition allows for vendors without such an agreement. Therefore, the change introduces an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 1.2 “Deliverables” means the physical goods, to be supplied and/ or services rendered in terms of the Order ;and shall include any activity, work and/or services to be rendered in conjunction with the Delivery of goods and vice versa;
perturbation.changed_text: 1.2 “Deliverables” means only the physical goods to be supplied in terms of the Order. Any services, activities, or work related to the goods are explicitly excluded from this definition.
perturbation.explanation: This change narrows the definition of 'Deliverables' to *only* physical goods, excluding related services, activities, or work. This creates a direct in-text contradiction with the original definition which included these elements. It also contradicts subsequent sections that discuss 'rendering Deliverables' because 'rendering' implies services, which are now excluded from the definition of 'Deliverables.' Vendor may be required to quote on and / or render Deliverables to Grindrod SA.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Vendor and Grindrod SA agree that; 1. The Vendor and Grindrod SA have entered into an agreement / understanding / request for quotation / contract / order or any interaction of whatsoever nature, under which the Vendor may be required to quote on and / or render Deliverables to Grindrod SA and/or Grindrod SA shall be obliged to disclose or otherwise make available to the Vendor, Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text narrows the definition of 'Deliverables' to exclude services, which directly contradicts the 'Contradicted Text' which explicitly states that the Vendor may be required to 'render Deliverables', and rendering implies services. This creates a direct conflict within the document based on the provided Perturbation Explanation, as the definition of Deliverables is altered in a way that breaks consistency and makes enforcement unclear.

perturbation.original_text: 3.6 that the provisions of this Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Undertaking shall survive the termination or expiration of any Agreement / understanding / Request for Quotation / Contract / Purchase Order or any interaction, with Grindrod SA, of whatsoever nature.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.6 that the provisions of this Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Undertaking shall survive the termination or expiration of any Agreement / understanding / Request for Quotation / Contract / Purchase Order, but shall not survive the termination of Vendor’s employment or contract with its direct employer or customer of Grindrod SA.
perturbation.explanation: This change adds a condition to the survival clause. Previously, the NDA survived *any* termination. Now, it does *not* survive the termination of the Vendor's employment or contract with its direct employer/customer of Grindrod SA. This directly contradicts the original survival clause and creates an in-text ambiguity. For example, after the Vendor is no longer directly employed by or contracted to the Customer, the Vendor can disclose Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1. to use such Confidential Information only for purposes of performing as required in terms of its obligations / promises or duties arising out of the Agreement / understanding /Request for Quotation / Contract or Purchase Order and for no other purpose whatsoever,
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies clause 3.6 to limit the survival of the Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Undertaking, stating it will not survive the termination of Vendor's employment/contract. This directly contradicts clause 3.1, which requires the Vendor to use Confidential Information only for performing obligations under the Agreement, implying this obligation extends beyond the termination of Vendor's employment/contract. The modification introduces a clear inconsistency, creating a strong in-text contradiction by allowing the Vendor to disclose Confidential Information after termination of employment/contract, while the original clause implied continued confidentiality for purposes relating to prior obligations.
